votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the logic of the Coq specification, checking that the string length is at least 3 and that all windows of 3 consecutive characters contain distinct characters.
The Python code correctly implements the logic of the Coq specification, checking if the string length is at least 3 and all consecutive triplets of characters are distinct.
The Python code correctly implements the logic of the Coq specification, including the bounds checking for the three-consecutive-distinct condition and the overall happy string criteria.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec. For three_consecutive_distinct, it checks if indices are valid and returns whether all three characters at positions i, i+1, i+2 are pairwise distinct. For is_happy_spec, it returns true iff result matches the expected value: false if length < 3, otherwise true only if all consecutive triples are distinct. The boundary check 'i + 2 >= len(s)' in Python correctly handles the Coq condition 'i + 2 < length s', and the loop 'range(len(s) - 2)' correctly iterates over all valid starting positions. The spec verification logic matches the Coq biconditional structure.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. The three_consecutive_distinct function checks if three consecutive characters at index i are all distinct. The is_happy_spec function returns true iff the result matches the expected value: true when length >= 3 and all consecutive triples are distinct, false otherwise. The boundary condition i + 2 < length s in Coq corresponds to range(len(s) - 2) in Python, which iterates i from 0 to len(s)-3 inclusive, matching the Coq condition. The Python i + 2 >= len(s) check in three_consecutive_distinct is slightly different (uses >= instead of >) but since is_happy_spec only calls it with valid indices from range(len(s) - 2), this doesn't affect results.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. The three_consecutive_distinct function checks if three consecutive characters at index i are all distinct. The is_happy_spec function returns true iff the result matches the expected value: true when length >= 3 and all consecutive triples are distinct, false otherwise. The boundary check i + 2 >= len(s) in Python's three_consecutive_distinct is slightly stricter than Coq's i + 2 < length s condition, but in is_happy_spec the loop range(len(s) - 2) ensures i + 2 < len(s), so the behavior matches. The Coq spec's biconditional structure is correctly captured by checking expected == result.

Both require result to be true iff len(s) >= 3 and every consecutive triple has pairwise distinct elements, and false otherwise; the Python code checks exactly this and returns whether the given result matches.

Python computes the same condition: result is true iff len(s) >= 3 and every consecutive triple has pairwise distinct elements; otherwise false. It returns True exactly when the given result matches this. The helper mirrors Coq’s nth_error behavior by returning False on out-of-bounds.

Python checks that result equals (len(s) >= 3 and all length-3 windows are pairwise distinct), which matches the Coq spec where the false case is the exact negation. The helper three_consecutive_distinct also mirrors the nth_error-based definition.
